**** (302) CONTINUED - PART 3**** MOREOVER, PLAINTIFF FAILS TO POINT TO ANY PORTION OF THE RECORD THAT WOULD CREATE A TRIABLE ISSUE OF FACT AS TO PLAINTIFF'S SUPERVISOR'S ALLEGED CONDUCT HAVING BEEN OUTRAGEOUS BEYOND THE BOUNDS OF HUMAN DECENCY OR THAT PLAINTIFF EXPERIENCED SEVERE EMOTIONAL SUFFERING. IN ADDITION, THE COURT CONSIDERED PLAINTIFF'S COUNSEL'S REQUEST THAT THE COURT RECONSIDER ITS TENTATIVE RULING REGARDING THE GRANT OF DEFENDANT'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT ON THE 2ND & 3RD CAUSES OF ACTION (VIOLATION OF LABOR CODE SECTION 1102.5 & IIED). AS TO THE 2ND CAUSE OF ACTION THE COURT FINDS THAT PLAINTIFF HAS PRESENTED NO EVIDENCE THAT PLAINTIFF REASONABLY BELIEVED OR KNEW THAT HE WAS DISCLOSING A VIOLATION OF FEDERAL OR STATE LAW. AS TO THE 3RD CAUSE OF ACTION THE COURT FINDS THAT THE BASIS FOR ITS TENTATIVE RULING IS SOUND. DEFENDANT TO PROVIDE AN ORDER TO THE COURT CONSISTENT WITH THE RULING & AN ORDER REFLECTING THE RULINGS ON ITS OBJECTIONS TO PLAINTIFF'S DECLARATION.

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, IS CONTINUED FROM JUN-13-2011 TO LAW AND MOTION AT JUN-17-2011, 9:30 AM IN DEPT. 302. MR. WALLACE IS NOT AVAILABLE ON 6/13/11; BOTH PARTIES WERE NOTIFIED OF NEW DATE.

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. ARGUED AND THE COURT TOOK THE MATTER UNDER SUBMISSION. - AFTER HEARING, THE COURT RULED AS FOLLOWS: COURT ADOPTS TENTATIVE RULING AS FINAL - SUMMARY ADJUDICATION/SUMMARY JUDGMENT IS GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART. AS TO THE SECOND CAUSE OF ACTION (VIOLATION OF LABOR CODE 1102.5) THE MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION IS GRANTED. PLAINTIFF CANNOT SHOW THAT HE HAD A REASONABLE BELIEF THAT DEFENDANT WAS VIOLATING FEDERAL, STATE OF LOCAL LAW WHEN HE COMPLAINED ABOUT THE USE OF PESTICIDES. THERE IS NOT EVIDENCE TO SUPPORT PLAINTIFF'S CONTENTION THAT THE MANNER IN WHICH THE SUBJECT PESTICIDES WERE USED VIOLATED ANY APPLICABLE LAW OR THAT THERE IS ANY APPLICABLE LAWS GOVERNING THE SPRAYING OR THAT DEFENDANT VIOLATED ANY SUCH LAWS. MOREOVER, PLAINTIFF CANNOT SUPPORT A CLAIM THAT HE KNEW THAT HIS COMPLAINT WAS DISCLOSING A VIOLATION OF SUCH LAWS OR THAT HE REASONABLY BELIEVE THAT IT DID. AS TO THE FIRST CAUSE OF ACTION (VIOLATION OF LABOR CODE 6310, THE MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION IS DENIED. PLAINTIFF HAS RAISED A TRIABLE ISSUE OF MATERIAL FACT REGARDING WHETHER OR NOT THE DEFENDANT'S EMPLOYMENT DECISION WAS BASED ON HIS COMPLAINTS TO HIS SUPERVISOR ABOUT THE SPRAYING OF PESTICIDE SPRAYING. WHILE THE FINAL DECISION WAS MADE BY THE DEPARTMENT HEAD, PHIL GINSBURG, THAT DECISION WAS BASED UPON PERFORMANCE INFORMATION PASSED UP THE CHAIN OF COMMAND AT THE DEPARTMENT BEGINNING WITH PLAINTIFF'S SUPERVISOR TO WHOM PLAINTIFF MADE HIS COMPLAINTS. MOREOVER, SOME OF THE PERFORMANCE ISSUES RAISED TO MR. GINSBURG WERE NOT RAISED IN PLAINTIFF'S WRITTEN OR ORAL PERFORMANCE EVALUATIONS. ****SEE NEXT ENTRY FOR PART 2 OF THE RULING****

****(302) CONTINUED - PART 2**** WHILE SUCH EVIDENCE IS CIRCUMSTANTIAL, IT IS SUFFICIENT TO PERMIT A JURY TO INFER THAT PLAINTIFF'S SUPERVISOR RAISED THESE ISSUES IN RETALIATION FOR PLAINTIFF'S COMPLAINTS ABOUT THE PESTICIDES. CLARK V. CLAREMONT UNIVERSITY CENTER, (1992) 6 CAL. APP.4TH 667-60. AS TO THE THIRD CAUSE OF ACTION (INTENTIONAL INFLICTION OF EMOTIONAL DISTRESS), THE MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION IS GRANTED. FIRST, PERSONNEL MANAGEMENT ACTIVITY IS CATEGORICALLY NOT COGNIZABLE AS OUTRAGEOUS CONDUCT AS IS REQUIRED TO MAINTAIN SUCH A CAUSE OF ACTION, EVEN IF AN IMPROPER MOTIVATION LIES BEHIND SUCH ACTIVITY. JANKEN V. G.M. HUGHES ELECTRONICS ET AL., (1996) 46 CAL.APP.4TH 55, 80. SECOND, THIS CAUSE OF ACTION IS EXPLICITLY DERIVATIVE OF THE CAUSES OF ACTION FOR VIOLATIONS OF THE LABOR CODE AND SUCH DUPLICITY BARS A CAUSE OF ACTION FOR IIED AS A MATTER OF LAW. COUCH V. SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT (1995) 33 CAL.APP.4TH 1491, 1504. ****SEE NEXT ENTRY FOR PART 3 OF THE RULING****

REMOVED FROM MASTER JURY CALENDAR SET FOR JUN-27-2011 - NO APPEARANCES BY PLAINTIFF AND DEFENDANT. THE COURT ORDERED THE CASE DISMISSED. JUDGE: KATHERINE FEINSTEIN; CLERK: E. BURA; MATTER NOT REPORTED. (206)

MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Jun-27-2011 Dismissal - No Appearance

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Jun-17-2011 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION.

APPEARANCES: Robert F. Wallace, Esq. of Access Legal Services 510/891-9900 for pltf & opposing party; Lisa B. Berkowitz, Deputy City Atty 554-3825 for deft & moving party.

RULING: Argued and the Court took the matter under submission.
After hearing, the Court ruled as follows: Court adopts tentative ruling as final - Defendants' Motion for Summary Adjudication/Summary Judgment is granted in part and denied in part. As to the Second cause of action (Violation of Labor Code 1102.5) the motion for Summary Adjudication is granted. Plaintiff cannot show that he had a reasonable belief that Defendant was violating Federal, State of local law when he complained about the use of pesticides. There is not evidence to support Plaintiff's contention that the manner in which the subject pesticides were used violated any applicable law or that there is any applicable laws governing the spraying or that Defendant violated any such laws. Moreover, Plaintiff cannot support a claim that he knew that his complaint was disclosing a violation of such laws or that he reasonably believe that it did. As to the First cause of action (Violation of Labor Code 6310, the motion for Summary Adjudication is denied. Plaintiff has raised a triable issue of material fact regarding whether or not the Defendant's employment decision was based on his complaints to his supervisor about the spraying of pesticide spraying. While the final decision was made by the Department Head, Phil Ginsburg, that decision was based upon performance information passed up the chain of command at the Department beginning with Plaintiff's Supervisor to whom Plaintiff made his complaints. Moreover, some of the performance issues raised to Mr. Ginsburg were not raised in Plaintiff's written or oral performance evaluations. While such evidence is circumstantial, it is sufficient to permit a jury to infer that Plaintiff's Supervisor raised these issues in retaliation for Plaintiff's complaints about the pesticides. Clark v. Claremont University Center, (1992) 6 Cal. App.4th 667-60. As to the Third cause of action (Intentional Infliction of Emotional Distress), the motion for Summary Adjudication is granted. First, personnel management activity is categorically not cognizable as outrageous conduct as is required to maintain such a cause of action, even if an improper motivation lies behind such activity. Janken v. G.M. Hughes Electronics et al., (1996) 46 Cal.App.4th 55, 80. Second, this cause of action is explicitly derivative of the causes of action for violations of the Labor Code and such duplicity bars a cause of action for IIED as a matter of law. Couch v. San Juan Unified School District (1995) 33 Cal.App.4th 1491, 1504. Moreover, Plaintiff fails to point to any portion of the record that would create a triable issue of fact as to Plaintiff's Supervisor's alleged conduct having been outrageous beyond the bounds of human decency or that Plaintiff experienced severe emotional suffering. In addition, The Court considered plaintiff's counsel's request that the Court reconsider its tentative ruling regarding the grant of defendant's motion for summary judgment on the 2nd & 3rd causes of action (violation of labor code section 1102.5 & IIED). As to the 2nd cause of action the Court finds that plaintiff has presented no evidence that plaintiff reasonably believed or knew that he was disclosing a violation of federal or state law. As to the 3rd cause of action the Court finds that the basis for its tentative ruling is sound. Defendant should provide an order to the Court consistent with the tentative ruling and an order reflecting the Court's rulings on its objections to plaintiff's declaration - the Wallace declaration which were sustained in part & overruled in part. (302/LMG) Judge: Loretta M. Giorgi, Reporter:Karla Ellis-Davis, #12998
